name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلْعَـٰدِيَـٰتِ ضَبْحًا (١)
```

```english
By the snorting chargers, (1)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْمُورِيَـٰتِ قَدْحًا (٢)
```

```english
by the strikers of sparks [with their hoofs], (2)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْمُغِيرَٰتِ صُبْحًا (٣)
```

```english
by the raiders at dawn, (3)
```

```arabic
فَأَثَرْنَ بِهِۦ نَقْعًا (٤)
```

```english
raising therein a trail of dust, (4)
```

```arabic
فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِۦ جَمْعًا (٥)
```

```english
and cleaving therein a host! (5)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لِرَبِّهِۦ لَكَنُودٌ (٦)
```

```english
Indeed man is ungrateful to his Lord, (6)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ لَشَهِيدٌ (٧)
```

```english
and indeed he is [himself] witness to that! (7)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ لِحُبِّ ٱلْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ (٨)
```

```english
And indeed he is an avid lover of wealth. (8)
```

```arabic
۞ أَفَلَا يَعْلَمُ إِذَا بُعْثِرَ مَا فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ (٩)
```

```english
Does he not know, when what is [buried] in the graves is turned over, (9)
```

```arabic
وَحُصِّلَ مَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ (١٠)
```

```english
and what is [concealed] in the breasts is divulged, (10)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ رَبَّهُم بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّخَبِيرٌۢ (١١)
```

```english
on that day their Lord will be well informed about them [and their deeds]? (11)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions man’s ingratitude toward the blessings of his Lord and his intense love for worldly goods (الخير) despite his underlying knowledge of this, which stands as a proof against him and for which he will be held accountable.

The Surah is Medinan (مدنية), as evidenced by the opening oaths such as His saying: **وَالْعادِياتِ ضَبْحاً** "By the snorting chargers," which clearly refers to the horses of the raiding warriors (المجاهدين), as will be explained. Jihad (الجهاد) — *the striving in the way of Allah* — was only legislated after the Migration (الهجرة). This is further supported by narrations from the Shi‘a through the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام) stating that the Surah was revealed concerning ‘Ali (عليه السلام) and his expedition in the Battle of Dhat al-Salasil. It is also supported by certain narrations from Sunni sources, as we shall indicate in the following "Discussion on Traditions," if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْعادِياتِ ضَبْحاً** "By the snorting chargers," *The chargers* (العاديات) is derived from *running* (العدو), which is to race swiftly. *Snorting* (الضبح) is the sound of the horses' breath during their run; this is a well-known characteristic of horses, even if it is claimed to occur in many other animals. The meaning is: I swear by the horses that run, snorting a distinct snort.

It has been said that it refers to the camels of the pilgrims as they hasten with their riders from Jam‘ to Mina on the Day of Sacrifice. Others said it refers to the camels of the warriors. However, the descriptions in the subsequent verses do not align with camels being the intended meaning of *the chargers* (العاديات).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَالْمُورِياتِ قَدْحاً** "by the strikers of fire," *Striking fire* (الإيراء) is to produce fire, and *striking* (القدح) is the well-known act of hitting or knocking. It is said: "He struck and produced fire" (قدح فأورى) when he brings forth fire by striking. The reference is to horses striking fire with their hooves when they run over stones and pebbly ground.

It has been said that *striking fire* (الإيراء) refers to the ruse of men in war; others said it refers to their lighting of fires; and some said it refers to the tongues of men "striking fire" due to the gravity of what they speak. These views are clearly weak.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَالْمُغِيراتِ صُبْحاً** "by the morning raiders," *Raiding* (الإغارة) and *the raid* (الغارة) is to launch a sudden attack on the enemy with horses. This is an attribute of the riders, and its attribution to the horses is metaphorical (مجاز). The meaning is: I swear by the horses that suddenly attack the enemy at the time of dawn.

It has been said that it refers to the camels hastening with their riders on the Day of Sacrifice from Jam‘ to Mina, as the Sunnah is not to depart until dawn. In this view, *raiding* (الإغارة) would mean speed of travel, which contradicts the apparent meaning of the word.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَثَرْنَ بِهِ نَقْعاً** "stirring therewith the dust," *Stirring* (أثَرْنَ) is from *excitation* (الإثارة), meaning to stir up dust and the like. *Dust* (النقع) is the dust itself. The meaning is: they stirred up dust through their running and raiding.

It has been said there is no issue with conjoining **فَأَثَرْنَ**, which is a verb, to what preceded it, which are adjectives, because the active participle (اسم فاعل) functions with the meaning of a verb. It is as if it were said: "I swear by those that ran, then struck fire, then raided, then stirred up dust."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِ جَمْعاً** "and plunging therewith into the midst of a host." *Plunging into the middle* (وسط) and *being in the middle* (توسط) carry the same meaning. The pronoun in **بِهِ** (therewith) refers to the morning (الصبح), with the preposition *bi* meaning "in"; or the pronoun refers to the dust (النقع), with the preposition denoting accompaniment (الملابسة).

The meaning is: they became, at the time of dawn, in the midst of a gathering—referring to the enemy's battalion. Or the meaning is: they mediated a gathering while being enveloped in dust.

It has been said that it refers to the camels mediating the gathering at Mina. You are well aware that interpreting these five verses—given the apparent meanings of their individual words—as the camels of the pilgrims pouring forth from Jam‘ to Mina is highly contrary to their apparent sense.

Thus, it is necessary to interpret them as the horses of the warriors. The context of the verses, especially His saying: **فَالْمُغِيراتِ صُبْحاً** "by the morning raiders" and **فَوَسَطْنَ بِهِ جَمْعاً** "and plunging therewith into the midst of a host," suggests a specific battle in which Allah swore by the horses of the *mujahidin* as they charged. The conjunction *fa* (ف) in the four verses indicates that each action follows sequentially from the one before it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لِرَبِّهِ لَكَنُودٌ** "Indeed man is ungrateful to his Lord," *Ungrateful* (الكنود) means disbelieving in blessings (الكفور). The verse is like His saying: **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَكَفُورٌ** "Indeed man is very ungrateful" (22:66). It is an informative statement regarding what is in the nature of man (طبع الإنسان) in terms of following whims, being engrossed in the fleeting goods of the world, and being cut off from thanking his Lord for the blessings He has bestowed upon him.

In this, there is an allusion to the people who were raided. It is as if the intended meaning of their ingratitude was their rejection of the blessing of Islam (نعمة الإسلام) which Allah bestowed upon them—the greatest blessing they were given, containing the goodness of their worldly life and the felicity of their eternal life hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّهُ عَلى ذلِكَ لَشَهِيدٌ** "and indeed he is a witness to that." The apparent consistency of the pronouns suggests that the pronoun in **وَإِنَّهُ** (and indeed he) refers to man. Thus, the meaning of his being a witness to his own ingratitude is his reprehensible knowledge of it and his bearing witness to it within himself.

The meaning is: indeed man is a witness and bearer of testimony to his own ingratitude toward his Lord. The verse is thus similar in meaning to His saying: **بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ** "Rather man is a witness against himself" (75:14).

It has been said that the pronoun refers to Allah, but the consistency of the pronouns does not support this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّهُ لِحُبِّ الْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ** "and indeed he is passionate in his love of wealth." It has been said that the *lam* in **لِحُبِّ الْخَيْرِ** is for causality, and *wealth* (الخير) refers to money. The meaning would be: indeed man, for the sake of the love of money, is *intense* (شديد), meaning miserly and stingy. Others said the meaning is that man is intense in his love for money, and this drives him to withhold the right of Allah and to refrain from spending for the sake of Allah. This is how they have interpreted it.

It is not improbable that *wealth* (الخير) refers to "good" in an absolute sense, and the meaning is that the love of good is part of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — of man. However, he then perceives the fleeting goods and ornaments of the world as "good," so his soul is attracted to them, causing him to forget to thank his Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَلا يَعْلَمُ إِذا بُعْثِرَ ما فِي الْقُبُورِ** "Does he not know when what is in the graves is overturned," until His saying: **لَخَبِيرٌ** "is indeed fully aware." *Overturning* (البعثرة) is like *scattering* (البحثرة), meaning to resurrect and spread out. *Bringing out what is in the breasts* (تحصيل ما في الصدور) is the distinguishing of what is within the souls in terms of the attributes of faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر), and the imprints of good and evil deeds. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ** "on the day when the secrets are examined" (86:9). It has also been said that it is the manifestation of what the breasts concealed so that one may be recompensed for the secret just as one is recompensed for the manifest.

His saying: **أَفَلا يَعْلَمُ** "Does he not know," the interrogation here is for rebuke (الإنكار). The object of "know" is a sentence standing in place of the two objects, as indicated by the context. Then a new sentence begins: "when what is in the graves is overturned..." as an emphasis of the rebuke. What is intended by "what is in the graves" are the bodies.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Does man not know that for his ingratitude and disbelief in his Lord there is a consequence that will overtake him and for which he will be recompensed? When the bodies are brought out from the graves, and what is in the secrets of the souls—faith, disbelief, obedience, and disobedience—is extracted and distinguished, indeed their Lord, on that day, is fully aware of them, and He will recompense them for what is within them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma‘*, it is said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent an expedition to a tribe of Kinanah, appointing al-Mundhir ibn ‘Amr al-Ansari, one of the leaders, over them. Their return was delayed, so the hypocrites said: "They have all been killed." Then Allah the Exalted informed about them by His saying: **وَالْعادِياتِ ضَبْحاً** (By the snorting chargers). This is narrated from Muqatil.

It is also said: The Surah was revealed when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent ‘Ali (عليه السلام) to Dhat al-Salasil and he defeated them. This occurred after he had sent other companions several times, and each of them had returned to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) [without success]. This is what is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) in a lengthy tradition.

He said: This battle was named Dhat al-Salasil (That of the Chains) because he captured, killed, and took prisoners from them, and their prisoners were bound in ropes, their hands tied behind their backs as if they were in chains.

When the Surah was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out to the people and led them in the morning prayer, reciting **وَالْعادِياتِ** (The Chargers). When he finished his prayer, his companions said: "This is a Surah we do not recognize." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Yes, indeed ‘Ali has triumphed over the enemies of Allah, and Gabriel brought me the glad tidings of that this very night." ‘Ali (عليه السلام) arrived a few days later with the spoils and the prisoners.
